title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a professional tea taster called Thomas Thorne talking about his career and the process of evaluating different types of tea. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a professional tea taster called Thomas Thorne talking about his career and the process of evaluating different types of tea. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Thomas Thorne describes the sensory skills and travel involved in being a tea sommelier.
extracts.audio_script:
Good morning. My name is Thomas, and I have what many consider to be the best job in the world: I’m a professional tea taster. People often imagine me sitting in a cozy armchair all day, but it’s actually a highly disciplined and technical role. My interest in tea didn't come from a family business; it started when I took a [7]gap year trip[/7] to Sri Lanka. I visited a plantation in the highlands and was blown away by the complexity of the flavors produced by a single plant species. I realized then that tea is as complex as fine wine.

To become a taster, you need a highly developed palate. I spent five years as an apprentice, learning to distinguish between hundreds of different grades. The first thing we look at is the dry leaf—its color, size, and even the sound it makes when you run your hand through it. But the real test begins with the infusion. We have a very strict protocol. We always use a [8]porcelain bowl[/8] for tasting because it doesn't retain flavors from previous sessions. It provides a neutral background that allows the true character of the tea to shine through.

During a typical morning session, I might taste up to two hundred different samples. We don't actually swallow the tea; like wine tasters, we spit it out to avoid a massive caffeine overdose. I found that the [9]slurping technique[/9] is the most important skill to master. By drawing air into your mouth along with the tea, you spray the liquid over all your taste buds and into the back of your nose. This allows you to perceive the 'nose' or aroma of the tea more clearly. It’s not very polite at a dinner party, but it’s essential in the lab.

One of the biggest threats to quality is how the tea is stored before it reaches us. Tea is incredibly absorbent. If it’s kept near a shipment of spices or even soap, it will ruin the flavor. I’ve found that [10]excessive moisture[/10] is the most common reason for a batch being rejected. It causes the leaves to go 'dull' and lose their crisp, energetic profile. We have to be very strict with our suppliers about their transport conditions.

My job also involves a lot of travel to remote areas. I spend about three months a year visiting growers in India, China, and Kenya. Building relationships with these farmers is vital. I’ve noticed that the [11]soil composition[/11] in certain high-altitude regions produces a unique mineral quality that you just can't find anywhere else. It’s a bit like 'terroir' in winemaking. Seeing the passion of the pickers, who often work in difficult terrain, makes me value every cup even more.

In terms of lifestyle, I have to be very careful. I can't eat spicy food or wear strong cologne before a session, as it would interfere with my senses. I think the most challenging thing for me personally was giving up [12]black coffee[/12]. It’s so powerful that it can numb your ability to detect the subtle floral notes in a light green tea for several hours. It was a difficult sacrifice, but necessary for the job.

As the market changes, we are seeing more interest in 'specialty' teas. Consumers are becoming much more knowledgeable and are asking for specific harvests. This has led to a [13]global shortage[/13] of certain rare white teas, which only grow in very specific weather conditions. It’s my job to find alternatives that offer a similar flavor profile without the astronomical price tag.

Finally, for anyone interested in this career, I am currently helping to develop a [14]certification program[/14] for the International Tea Association. It’s designed to standardize the tasting language we use across the industry. It’s a fascinating world, and I hope more people discover the joy of a truly great cup of tea. Thank you.
